coredata.dc:title: Enhancing maritime corrosion resistance of epoxy coating on steels by using rust conversion and graphene-based composites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Composites Part A: Applied Science and Manufacturing

coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Ziyang
Wang, Zhengquan
Niu, Ranming
Huang, Siyu
Cairney, Julie M.
Fan, Xueling
Chen, Eason Yi-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Rust conversion and composite coatings offer effective solutions for enhancing corrosion resistance in structural materials used in marine environments. They are both cost-effective and easy to implement. In this study, we introduce a novel coating that combines tannic acid-based iron-rust conversion with graphene-based nanocomposites, resulting in improved corrosion resistance for epoxy coatings on steel surfaces. We employed a variety of characterization techniques to assess the superior anti-corrosion and structural properties of these new coatings, including X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy with focused ion beam and energy dispersion X-ray spectrometer, transmission electron microscopy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, as well as mechanical testing. The development of the new materials holds promise for environmental benefits and widespread industrial applications of nanocomposites in the field of rust conversion coatings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rust conversion coating
Tannic acid
Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Nanohybrid
Anti-corrosion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots, nanohybrid, and tannic acid functionalization
Rust layers before and after tannic acid treatment
Nanohybrids of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the funding from Australian Research Council (LP180100431, LP210300999, and FT180100232), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M742782), and Singapore National Research Foundation (NRF-NRFF16-2024-0009). Additionally, they acknowledge the research facilities and technical service supported by Microscopy Australia and the University of Sydney\u2019s Core Research Facility program.

The authors thank the funding from Australian Research Council (LP180100431, LP210300999, and FT180100232), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M742782), and Singapore National Research Foundation (NRF-NRFF16-2024-0009). Additionally, they acknowledge the research facilities and technical service supported by Microscopy Australia and the University of Sydney's Core Research Facility program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the synthetization of GO@GQDs nanohybrids and its TA-functionalized counterpart. Rusted steel substrate, pure epoxy, and epoxy-based nanocomposite coatings.

Morphologies and compositions of steel rust layers. (a)-(c) are optical images of unattached rust, attached rust, and steel substrate with attached rust after tannic acid treatment, respectively. (d) show the XRD patterns of all the rust/TA samples. (e) and (f) are the SEM images and EDS analyses of the attached rust and steel substrate with attached rust after tannic acid treatment, respectively.
XRD patterns of (a) TA-GO and TA-GQDs, (b) GO@GQDs and TA-GO@GQDs. FTIR results of (c) TA-GO and TA-GQDs, (d) GO@GQDs and TA-GO@GQDs.
XPS analyses for GO@GQDs and TA-GO@GQDs: (a) survey scan, (b) high resolution C 1s, (c) high resolution O 1s, and (d) high resolution N 1s.
TEM images of (a) GO@GQDs and (b) TA-GO@GQDs.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analyses of the PE/TA (red), PE/TA-GO (blue), PE/TA-GQDs (yellow), and PE/TA-GO@GQDs (green) samples after the corrosion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for (a) 1 day and (b) 28 days. (c) The corresponding impedance |Z| at frequency of 0.01 Hz.
(a) The corresponding models for the equivalent electrical circuits; the changes in (b) Rpore, (c) Rct, (d) Qc, and (e) Qdl values over time of immersion for various coatings.
SEM-EDS results from the scratch area of the PE/TA, PE/TA-GO, PE/TA-GQDs, and PE/TA-GO@GQDs samples.

The measurements of (a) water contact angle and (b) surface roughness by laser scanning confocal microscope on the PE/TA, PE/TA-GO, PE/TA-GQDs, and PE/TA-GO@GQDs samples before and after immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 20 days.
The images of the pull-off adhesion test for the (a) PE/TA, (b) PE/TA-GO, (c) PE/TA-GQDs, and (d) PE/TA-GO@GQDs coatings to the metallic substrate.
The SEM images from the fracture surface of the (a) PE/TA, (b) PE/TA-GO, (c) PE/TA-GQDs, and (d) PE/F-GO@GQDs coatings.
SEM/EDS analysis from the surface of the PE/TA (left) and PE/TA-GO@GQDs (right) after FIB ion slicing.
The mechanism for the rust conversion and corrosion protection of the PE/TA-GO@GQDs coating on the steel substrate.

head.ce:title: Enhancing maritime corrosion resistance of epoxy coating on steels by using rust conversion and graphene-based composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rust conversion and composite coatings offer effective solutions for enhancing corrosion resistance in structural materials used in marine environments. They are both cost-effective and easy to implement. In this study, we introduce a novel coating that combines tannic acid-based iron-rust conversion with graphene-based nanocomposites, resulting in improved corrosion resistance for epoxy coatings on steel surfaces. We employed a variety of characterization techniques to assess the superior anti-corrosion and structural properties of these new coatings, including X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy with focused ion beam and energy dispersion X-ray spectrometer, transmission electron microscopy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, as well as mechanical testing. The development of the new materials holds promise for environmental benefits and widespread industrial applications of nanocomposites in the field of rust conversion coatings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion poses a significant threat to metal structures, causing deterioration that raises serious safety and economic concerns [1–3]. It results in the formation of rust and its subsequent penetration into the substrate, exacerbating the damage. While coatings are commonly used to protect against corrosion, many struggle to adhere well with the intrinsic rust on the metal surface and provide adequate protection for corroded structures.

In steels for maritime applications, tannic acid (TA) has recently become popular for iron rust conversion due to its strong chelation effect with metal ions [4–7]. This allows it to interact effectively with iron oxides and oxyhydroxides, forming stable ferric tannates [8–10]. Using tannic acid in rust conversion treatment improves the compactness of the rust layer, enhancing adhesion between coatings and rusty substrates [11]. Moreover, tannic acid is environmentally friendly, making it suitable as a green corrosion inhibitor [12,13].

Meanwhile, various organic and inorganic nanomaterials (e.g., graphene oxide (GO), metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), and metal oxides) have been incorporated into polymer coatings, including self-healing and active anti-corrosion coatings [14]. These coatings have attracted considerable scientific attention due to their broad range of potential applications and their demonstrated ability to significantly enhance long-term anti-corrosion performance [15–18]. Literature has shown that the addition of GO can lead to the formation of a dense protective barrier in polymer coatings, inhibiting the permeation of corrosive ions [19–22]. On the other hand, GO nanosheets tend to aggregate within the polymer matrix due to van der Waals interactions and π-π stacking, which significantly limits their potential applications [23]. To address this issue, inorganic particles such as graphene quantum dots, zinc oxide quantum dots, or silicon dioxide can be employed to modify the GO surface through active site [24–26]. This hybrid approach increases the interlayer spacing of GO nanosheets, enhances the bridging interactions between GO and the organic matrix, and improves their dispersion within the coating, thereby significantly enhancing the overall performance of the composite material. In our previous studies, various GO-based nanohybrid materials exhibited improved multifunctional performance when incorporated into waterborne epoxy coatings [27–29].

Additionally, among the above mentioned inorganic nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with their small particle size and functional groups can chemically bond with both the metallic substrate and polymer matrix, further enhancing corrosion resistance [30]. Functionalized GQDs have been shown to significantly improve the corrosion resistance of polymer coatings by forming a dense barrier against corrosive agents [31,32]. In this realm, Pourhashem et al. [33] demonstrated the effectiveness of silane-functionalized GQDs on enhancing the corrosion resistance of solvent-based composite coatings.

In this work, we have developed a pure epoxy-based nanocomposite coating, modified with tannic acid and incorporating nanosized graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots (PE/TA-GO@GQDs), to convert rust on corroded steel surfaces in simulated marine environment. Tannic acid, chosen for its environmental friendliness, acts as both a rust converter and a modifying agent for material compatibility. The combined effect of GO, GQDs, and TA on rust substrates provides both theoretical and technical insights into preserving and restoring deteriorated metal structures. This innovative approach, merging nanotechnology with rust conversion coatings, offers a new strategy for addressing corrosion challenges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots, nanohybrid, and tannic acid functionalization
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous solution containing graphene oxide (10 mg/mL) used in this study was acquired from Suzhou Tanfeng Graphene Technology Co. LTD, China. To synthesize graphene quantum dots, a hydrothermal method was employed. Initially, a mixture of 2.94 g of citric acid and 2.52 g of urea was dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water at room temperature and stirred magnetically for 30 min. The resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon-coated autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. The hydrothermal product obtained was then dried at 80 °C and finely ground using an agar mortar to obtain graphene quantum dots in powder form.

The nanohybrid material, comprising graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots (referred to as GO@GQDs in this work), was synthesized using a similar hydrothermal method. Initially, a solution of GO (10 mg/mL, 50 mL) was combined with 20 mL of ethanol and sonicated for 50 min. Then, 0.5 g of the synthesized GQDs were added to the mixture, followed by an additional 60 min of sonication. The resulting solution was magnetically stirred at room temperature for 60 min, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and subjected to heat treatment at 120 °C for 12 h [34]. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture underwent centrifugation and washing with ethanol and water, yielding GO@GQDs as precipitates, which were then dried in an oven at 60 °C.

The tannic acid used for rust conversion was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, China, and was subsequently employed to functionalize the nanomaterial. As detailed in [35], this process involves dissolving 4 g of tannic acid in 100 mL of deionized water using magnetic stirring and sonication. Then 0.2 g of nanomaterials was added to the solution, followed by centrifugation, washing, and drying at 80 °C before characterization. The tannic acid-functionalized GO, GQDs, and their hybrids were denoted as TA-GO, TA-GQDs, and TA-GO@GQDs, respectively. Fig. 1 depicts the main components and steps involved in obtaining the PE/TA-GO@GQDs epoxy-based nanocomposite coating.

Q235 steel substrates were cleaned with ethanol and allowed to air-dry before being immersed in a 3.5 % NaCl solution for 20 min. They were then placed in an environmental chamber maintained at 35 °C with a relative humidity of 96 % to promote the formation of a uniform rust layer on the steel surface. After the pre-corrosion phase, any loose rust was manually removed through hand-brushing, leaving only the firmly adhered rust layer. To ensure the conversion of all red rust to black, the rusted surface underwent two brushings with a tannic acid solution.

The waterborne epoxy resin (MU-618, epoxy equivalent = 200–220 g/mol, solid content = 50 ± 2 %, and solid epoxy density = 1.62 g/cm3) and its curing agent (CU-600, amine value = 180–190 mg KOH/g) were obtained from Runtan New Materials Technology of Shanghai Co., Ltd., China. Ethanol, sodium chloride, acetone, citric acid, and urea were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company in Shanghai, China. Epoxy-based coating solutions containing 100 g of epoxy resin and 0.25 % of designated nanomaterials in 10 mL of deionized water were prepared and stirred for 15 min, followed by 10 min of sonication until homogeneous. Subsequently, 50 g of hardener was added to the mixture, which underwent a degassing process under a vacuum oven at room temperature before application onto the rusted metallic substrate. The coating was allowed to air-dry at room temperature for 7 days, followed by final drying in an oven for 4 h at 80 °C. Coatings with a thickness of 200 ± 10 μm, measured using a GTS8202 digital coating thickness gauge (Guangzhou GuoOu Electronic Technology Co.), were utilized for further characterization. Additionally, material performance was compared with pure epoxy (PE) as a reference.

The morphologies of the nanomaterials before and after tannic acid functionalization were analyzed using various techniques. Initially, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using an X'Pert3 diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical). The scan spanned a 2θ range from 5 to 80°, with a scanning speed of 10 degree/min used for identifying crystalline structures. Subsequently, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Nicolet iS50) was utilized to analyze chemical structures in the wavelength range of 500 to 4500 cm−1. a JEOL JEM-2100Plus high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) was employed to observe the morphologies of the nanomaterials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, EscaLab 250Xi) was used to assess the chemical composition of the synthetic nanomaterials. Additionally, water contact angle measurements were conducted on all fabricated nanocomposite samples using an OCA25 contact angle meter. The surface roughness of all coating samples was assessed using laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM, Olympus LEXT OLS5000), while the adhesion strength was measured using PosiTest AT-M equipment. The fracture surfaces of these coatings were examined using a Regulus 8100 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Prior to SEM observations, the samples were coated with gold for 30 s.

For assessing corrosion resistance, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed. An AMETEK PARSTAT 4000A Potentiostat Galvanostat was used as the electrochemical workstation. The electrochemical cell used included a platinum electrode as the counter electrode, the coating/steel samples as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a corrosive electrolyte consisting of 3.5 % NaCl solution. EIS data collection occurred when the test samples reached a steady-state open circuit potential (OCP) within the frequency range of 105 – 10−2 Hz, using an amplitude of 20 mV. To further evaluate the nanocomposite coatings' ability to prevent corrosion, samples with scribes were subjected to a salt spray experiment following the ASTM B117 standard [36]. Additionally, the buried microstructures of the coatings were investigated using a Thermo Fisher G4 Hydra Plasma focused ion beam (FIB)-SEM system, integrated with an Oxford Instrument AZtec EDS detector. PFIB was utilized for surface removal, with a selected area measuring 20 × 20 μm2. A depth of 25 μm was removed during each iteration. Subsequently, a final cleaning process at 5 kV was conducted to eliminate any Xe damage incurred during the milling process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rust layers before and after tannic acid treatment
ce:section.ce:para: In the salt spray chamber, steel substrate undergone corrosion formed a layered rust. This rust comprises two distinct layers: an outer layer of loosely attached rust, as shown in Fig. 2 (a), and an inner layer of rust firmly adhered to the substrate, as shown in Fig. 2(b). To prepare the steel substrate for further treatment, the outer layer of rust, referred as unattached rust hereafter, was carefully removed using a metal brush, leaving behind only the adherent inner rust layer, referred as attached rust hereafter. Subsequently, the remaining rust layer on the steel substrate was subjected to the tannic acid treatment to form a protective black film on the steel surface, as shown in Fig. 2(c).

Further analysis of the steel surfaces was conducted through X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD results are presented in Fig. 2(d) and reveal the constituent phases present on the steel surfaces. Specifically, the surface with the attached rust layer shows the presence of predominantly iron (Fe) and magnetite (Fe3O4) on the surface, which is consistent with findings documented in existing literature [37]. Following the tannic acid treatment to the steel substrate with the attached rust, the XRD analysis shows a similar pattern as depicted in black. This pattern exhibits peaks corresponding to Fe) Fe3O4, mirroring those observed on the surface with the attached rust. This similarity suggests that the compositions of the two surfaces, before and after tannic acid treatment, are alike. In contrast, the XRD data obtained from the corrosion product removed from the steel surface by the metal brush, i.e., the unattached rust, reveals a pristine rust composition. This composition includes compounds such as Fe2O3·H2O (lepidocrocite), FeOOH, and Fe2O3·H2O (goethite), as identified by their respective JCPDS references. These findings are consistent with previous literature on rust composition [8,38,39].

Fig. 2(e) and (f) provide a comparative analysis of the surface morphologies and chemical compositions of the steel surfaces with attached rust and those treated with tannic acid. The SEM micrograph on the left side of Fig. 2(e) shows that the surface bearing attached rust exhibits greater roughness, and more cracks compared to its tannic acid-treated counterpart, as shown on the left side of Fig. 2(f). The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) results presented on the right side of Fig. 2(e) indicate that the steel surface with attached rust contains 31.9 % iron and 36.4 % oxygen by weight. In contrast, the tannic acid-treated rust surface displays a smoother corrosion product surface, with 76.1 % iron and 14.8 % oxygen by weight. While EDS is primarily suited for qualitative analysis, the substantial increase in the iron-to-oxygen ratio following tannic acid treatment serves as a good indication of compositional alteration resulting from the treatment, which is likely due to a chemical conversion of the rust layer as documented in literature [5]. According to literature [4], the reaction between the tannic acid and the rust layer can be explained by the following reactions, with an initial step involves the dissolution of the iron rust layer, resulting in the formation of Fe3+:
(1) γ-Fe2O3 + 6H+ → 2Fe3++ 3H2O

Then the tannic acid can penetrate through the initial rust layer, eventually contacting the steel substrate, resulting in the oxidation of iron, initiating a subsequent reaction between the iron ions and the tannic acid, which can be described as follows:
(2) Fe → Fe2+ + 2e−
(3) Tannins → tannate + ne−+nH+

Then the reaction of tannic acid with Fe2+ and Fe3+ can take place as:
(4) Tannate + nFe3+→ (Fe3+)n tannate
(5) Tannate + nFe2+= (Fe2+)n tannate
(6) nFe3+ + tannins → (Fe2+)n tannate + nH+

The formation of (Fe2+)n tannate can then be oxidized in the presence of oxygen, resulting in the formation of ferric-tannates:
(7) (Fe2+)n tannate + 1/4nO2 + nH+→ (Fe3+)n tannate + 1/2 nH2O
(8) (1/2O2 + 2H+ + 2e−→ H2O) × n/2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanohybrids of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns for both tannic-acid-treated GO and GQDs samples are depicted in Fig. 3 (a). For the pristine, untreated GO sample, a distinct peak is observed at 10.8° of 2θ, as discussed in our previous study [34]. However, for the tannic-acid-treated GO (TA-GO), as illustrated in Fig. 3(a), this peak shifts significantly to approximately 25° of 2θ and appears broader. This shift can be attributed to the reduction effect of tannic acid on the GO structure. Specifically, tannic acid may reduce epoxy and some carboxyl groups on the surface of graphene oxide, thereby making its structure more similar to that of graphene, as documented in literature [40]. The TA-GQDs sample exhibits a similar XRD pattern to TA-GO, suggesting the similar structure of the tannic-acid-treated GO and GQDs.

Fig. 3(b) presents the XRD patterns of the graphene oxide-graphene quantum dots hybrid sample (GO@GQDs, red) and tannic acid-treated counterpart (TA-GO@GQDs), revealing a broad peak centered at approximately 25° of 2 theta for both samples, corresponding to the (002) diffraction plane of the graphite structure. This broad peak is attributed to the reduction effect of hydrothermal treatment during the synthesis of GO@GQDs nanohybrids, as well as the decoration of the surface of GO nanosheets with zero-dimensional GQDs, as documented in literature [40,41]. In summary, the similar XRD patterns of the GO@GQDs samples before and after tannic acid treatment suggest the treatment does not alter the structures of the nanomaterials significantly.

Fig. 3(c) display the FTIR results from both tannic acid-treated GO and GQDs samples Specifically, the FTIR spectrum of TA-GO shows characteristic peaks (that suggest the presence of corresponding chemical bonds) at 3270 cm−1 (OH), 1709 cm−1 (C=O), 1606 cm−1 (C=C), 1531 cm−1 (C=C), 1449 cm−1 (C=O), 1322 cm−1 (C-O), 1199 cm−1 (C-O-C), 1086 cm−1 (C-O-C), and 1030 cm−1 (C-O-C). These peaks are also presence in the FTIR spectrum of TA-GQDs, suggesting the similarity of the GO and GQDs in chemical structure, which is consistent with the fundings in our previous research [28,42] and the XRD results in Fig. 3(a). In Fig. 3(d), the FTIR results from the GO@GQDs and TA-GO@GQDs samples are compared, suggesting the similar chemical structures of the nanomaterials before and after tannic acid treatment. Specifically, in Fig. 3(d), the GO@GQDs sample shows peaks (and chemical bonds) at 3136 cm−1 (OH), 1650 cm−1 (C=C), 1580 cm−1 (C=C), 1403 cm−1 (C=O), 1327 cm−1 (C-O), and 1113 cm−1 (C-O-C), which are similar to those observed for the TA-GO@GQDs sample. These results suggest the tannic acid treatment did not change the chemical structure of the nanomaterials, which is in good agreement with the XRD results shown in Fig. 3(b).

Fig. 4 (a) presents the XPS analyses of GO@GQDs and tannic acid-treated GO@GQDs, revealing the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) in the GO@GQDs sample. In contrast, the TA-GO@GQDs sample contains only the carbon and oxygen peaks without the nitrogen peak. Fig. 4(b) displays the details of the 1 s peak of carbon from 282 to 292 eV. Both TA-GO@GQDs and GO@GQDs samples exhibit three peaks: C=C/C–C, C=O/C-O, and COOH/O-C=O at 284, 286, and 288 eV, respectively, consistent with literature [43,44].

By comparing the spectra in Fig. 4(b), it can be seen that the C=O/C-O peak (red) is enhanced after tannic acid treatment. Fig. 4(c) displays the detailed view of the peaks appear near the 1s peak of oxygen from 528 to 536 eV. Both peaks from the GO@GQDs and TA-GO@GQDs results samples can be decomposed into two peaks that can be attributed to C-O and C-OH bonds at 532 and 533 eV, as reported in literature [45]. We then examined the 1s peak of nitrogen in the GO@GQDs (red) result in Fig. 4(a), as shown in Fig. 4(d). Deconvoluting the peak led to 4 peaks at 399 eV (blue) and 400 eV (red), corresponding to Pyrrolic-N, and those at 401.4 and 402 eV, corresponding to Graphitic-N, as reported in literature [46].

We then used TEM to examine the GO@GQDs nanohybrids before and after tannic acid treatment, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), respectively. In Fig. 5(a), a cluster of spherical GQDs is observed, which is presence on the surface of the two-dimensional GO. Fig. 5(b) shows the tannic acid-treated GO@GQDs, where the size of GQDs was found increased slightly with more even distribution on the GO surface. The results in Fig. 5 suggest no significant geometrical and structural changes of the GQDs were observed, confirming the stability of GQDs against the tannic acid treatment. We also examined the dispersion stability of the nanomaterials over time, as shown in Fig. S3 in the Supplementary Information, suggesting the good stability of the nanomaterials.

To quantitatively measure the corrosion resistance changes resulting from the addition of nanomaterials, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used for evaluating four pure epoxy-based (PE) tannic acid-treated (TA) coatings, containing either GO, GQDs, or their hybrid, during the saltwater corrosion. As shown in Fig. 6 , the data are denoted as PE/TA, PE/TA-GO, PE/TA-GQDs, and PE/TA-GO@GQDs, respectively. The samples were corroded in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 28 days, resulting in the data shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b), respectively. Fig. 6(a) and (b) exhibit the impedances (|Z|) and the phase angles as a function of signal frequency (using log scale), as shown in the top and bottom figures, respectively. The dots and solid in Fig. 6(a) and (b) represent the experimental data and the numerically fitted data, respectively, which used the experimental data, and the electrical equivalent circuits described in Fig. 7 .

The key information that can be extracted from Fig. 6(a) and (b) is the impedance at the lowest frequency, i.e., the |Z| at f = 0.01 Hz. For example, the value for PE/TA sample after 1 day of corrosion is 3.06 × 108 Ω·cm2, whereas the counterpart after 28 days of corrosion is decreased to 3.48 × 106 Ω·cm2. The low-frequency impedances for the four coatings are presented in Fig. 6(c) that shows a descending trend for those from the samples experiencing a longer period of corrosion as compared to those counterparts experiencing a shorter time. This decrease is likely due to the accumulation of mobile ionic corrosion product within the coating after a long-term corrosion, which increases the conductivity and reduces the overall resistance of the coatings.

Fig. 6(c) also exhibits that, among the four coatings, the PE/TA-GO@GQDs sample has the highest low-frequency impedance (1.51 × 1010 Ω·cm2) than those of the PE/TA-GQDs (1.11 × 109 Ω·cm2), PE/TA-GO (8.82 × 108 Ω·cm2), and PE/TA (3.06 × 108 Ω·cm2). Same trend can be found in the data from 28 days of corrosion. Another finding from Fig. 6(c) is that the low-frequency impedance for the coatings containing nanohybrid remains high after 28 days of corrosion, with respect to the PE/TA reference sample after 1 day of corrosion. The comparison in Fig. 6(c) suggests the effectiveness of the addition and hybridization of GO and GQDs for increasing corrosion resistance. The Nyquist plots corresponding to the impedance analyses are shown in Fig. S4 in the Supplementary Information for the readers of interest.

In addition to measuring low frequency impedance, by using ZSimpWin software to analyze the EIS data, we extracted the contribution responses from various components, including electron charge transfer resistance (Rct), double layer capacitance (Qdl), solution resistance (Rs), coating capacitance (Qc), pore resistance (Rpore) and other factors which are in direct contact with the corrosive solution, amidst multiple electrochemical processes operating at different reaction rates [47], as illustrated in Fig. 7(a). Moreover, the Warburg impedance (Zw) signifies the diffusion of electrolyte through the coating and the formation of corrosion products within the coating system [48]. Double layer capacitance may be related to the adhesion of the coating to the mental substrate. In this circuit, the polymer and TA-treated layer form a unified coating system. Namely, the properties relatable with the coatings are Rpore, Rct, Qc, and Qdl.

The extracted values of Rpore, Rct, Qc, and Qdl are shown in Fig. 7 (b)-(e), respectively. Overall, both the Rpore and Rct values of all coatings, i.e., Fig. 7(b) and (c), respectively, exhibit a declining trend with prolonged corrosion time, indicating that all coating systems undergo some degree of degradation and chemical reactions that lead to the decrease of corrosion resistance, or increase of potential to corroding the steel substrates. However, it is noteworthy that in comparison to the reference coating without nanomaterials, i.e., PE/TA, all the coatings added nanomaterials present lower extent of resistance reduction. Among the nanocomposite coatings, the PE/TA-GO@GQDs (the far-right dataset in the figures) exhibit the highest Rpore and Rct values, suggesting the effectiveness of the nanohybrids for increasing corrosion resistance.

In addition to resistance, the Qc and Qdl analyses, i.e., Fig. 7(d) and (e), respectively, show low values for the nanocomposite coatings, with PE/TA-GO@GQDs having the lowest value, suggesting enhanced adhesion of the coating to iron rust as well as the rust to the metallic substrate, as compared to the PE/TA reference. Overall, the EIS analyses indicate that the additions of GO and GQDs and the use of tannic acid treatment result an enhancement of the charge transfer resistance, which can lead to effective inhibition of corrosion. We also conducted visual observation on the results of salt spray test as shown in Fig. S5 in the Supplementary Information, confirming better performance of the coatings with nanohybrid in corrosion protection, comparing with the reference without adding nanomaterials.

We then performed SEM-EDS analysis on the scratched areas of the samples after the salt spray experiment to evaluate the impact of nanofillers on the corrosion of the metal substrate, as shown in Fig. 8 . Sodium and chlorine elements are identified as originating from the salt spray environment, while iron and oxygen elements are derived from the corrosion products (iron oxide or iron hydroxide) present in the metal substrate. In Fig. 8(a), the rust formed on the substrate within the scratch area of the PE/TA coating exhibits a corrosion product characterized by spherical morphology and a high oxygen content (approximately 31.1 %), with a lower iron content (approximately 63.9 %) being detected. Conversely, the scratched areas of the nanocomposite coatings in Fig. 8(b)-(d) show lower contents of the elements relating to corrosion products, particularly oxygen, chlorine, and sodium, as compared those of the PE/TA reference in Fig. 8(a). This observation highlights the higher resistance to the permeation of corrosion product in the nanocomposite coatings as compared to that of the counterpart. In recent years, scholars have proposed the concept of carbon negative footprint, which aims to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and achieve net-zero targets [49]. In this study, we utilized waterborne epoxy coatings and environmentally friendly nanomaterials to drive innovation and progress in the coating industry, contributing to the goal of achieving a carbon negative footprint.

The wettability of the coatings is evaluated by determining the water contact angles (WCA) on the prepared nanocomposite coating system. Fig. 9 presents the WCA on the PE/TA, PE/TA-GO, PE/TA-GQDs, and PE/TA-GO@GQDs coatings before and after 20 days of soaking in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Prior to immersion, the measured contact angles were 91.8 ± 3.1, 105.4 ± 1.6, 106.9 ± 0.9, and 102.7 ± 2.8 degrees, respectively. The incorporation of nanomaterials increased the hydrophobicity of the coating by approximately 15 %, indicating the influence of the nanomaterials' characteristics on the coating's surface performance. After a 20-day immersion period, the water contact angles of all coating systems showed a significant decrease due to their water absorption properties, resulting in angles of 67.8 ± 1.7, 86.0 ± 3.1, 91.8 ± 0.6, and 90.2 ± 1.8 degrees, respectively. Notably, the PE/TA-GO coatings exhibited the most pronounced decrease, possibly due to the hydrophilic groups within the waterborne epoxy coating and on the surface of GO. Conversely, the decline in WCA for the PE/TA-GO@GQDs coating was slower, indicating that it still retained hydrophobic properties contributing to its anti-corrosion performance. These WCA findings align well with the surface roughness result obtained from the laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) as shown in Fig. 10 (b). The root mean square surface roughness (Sq) analysis indicates that the presence of nanofillers in the coating could increase the Sq value although all values decreased with prolonged immersion time.

Adhesion strength is another crucial factor for assessing the quality of anti-corrosion coatings [50]. Examining the failure types of the pull-off adhesion strength can help understand the failure mechanism. The adhesion strengths of the PE/TA, PE/TA-GO, PE/TA-GQDs, and PE/TA-GO@GQDs coatings to the rusty substrate with tannic acid treatment are 1.3 ± 0.9, 3.3 ± 1.2, 4.1 ± 0.8, and 3.9 ± 0.5 MPa, respectively. It appears that the improvement in adhesion was facilitated by the formation of the micro-cracked tannic conversion layer and the increased compactness of the rust layer [51]. Fig. 10 shows a mixed fracture between the coatings and the treated rust layer, indicating that the combined addition of nanomaterials and TA effectively acts on the interface.

We then utilized SEM to inspect the fracture surface of the samples and the dispersion of nanomaterials within the epoxy matrix. This examination aimed to develop a link between the fracture morphologies of the coatings and their corrosion behaviors. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the pure epoxy sample shows many micropores and defects. Fig. 11(b) image from the PE/TA-GO coating exhibits the decrease of porosity in the sample containing the nanofillers. In Fig. 11(c) from a PE/TA-GQDs coating, we found a rougher surface with micro-ridges, comparing with the image of PE/TA-GO, which is indicative for a robust physical bonding between the GQDs and the tannic acid-treated epoxy matrix, consistent with literature [52]. Furthermore, in comparison to the PE/TA-GO and PE/TA-GQDs coatings, the PE/TA-GO@GQDs, as shown in Fig. 11(d), exhibit an even rougher surface, suggesting the strongest adhesion resulting from the addition of the nanohybrids and their good dispersion in the epoxy matrix. In summary, The fracture surface examination signifies a decrease of porosity, a good structural integrity of the coating with nanomaterials, and a high compatibility between the nanofiller and the epoxy matrix, which are consistent with literature [53].

We also used FIB/SEM ‘slice and view’ technique to investigate and compare the substructures buried in the PE/TA and PE/TA-GO@GQDs coatings that were immersed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for one week, as shown in the left and right columns in Fig. 12 , respectively. The top, middle, and bottom rows of images correspond to the ones taken from the surface, the substrate beneath surface by 25 μm, and those by 50 μm, respectively. We also obtained the compositional data using the EDS attached to the FIB. By comparing the EDS results in Fig. 12(a1) and (b1), we found the surfaces of PE/TA and PE/TA-GO@GQDs coatings show similar contents of chloride, which is considered as representative corrosive ions and is thus indicative for the penetration of corrosion. We also observed more pores in the PE/TA surface image, i.e., Fig. 12(a1). At 25 μm beneath the surface, i.e., Fig. 12(a2) and (b2), we found larger pores in the PE/TA coatings and higher chlorine content than that of PE/TA-GO@GQDs, suggesting a stronger resistance to corrosion product permeation and better densification in the coating with nanohybrids. At 50 μm beneath the surface, the relativities in porosity and chlorine content remain the same. We also found small pores in the PE/TA-GO@GQDs, which might be due to the decomposition of the nanomaterials after the FIB ion beam slicing. In summary, the SEM/FIB/EDS analysis allows the confirmation of strong resistance of lower porosity of the coating with nanofillers as compared to the PE/TA reference coating.

The obtained results suggest an effective rust conversion and enhanced corrosion resistance due to the addition of nanofiller into the waterborne epoxy coatings. The enhancement in corrosion resistance can be attributed to several factors, as illustrated in Fig. 13 . Firstly, pretreating the corroded substrate with tannic acid initiates a reaction in the loose rust layer, leading to the formation of a dense lamellar tannic iron structure with uniformly distributed micro-cracks on the surface. This process allows the solution to penetrate into the inner rust layer, densifying the previously porous layer before conversion and significantly improving the adhesion between the nanocomposite coating and the steel. Secondly, the incorporation of nanofillers effectively reinforces the barrier properties of the pure epoxy matrix, thereby enhancing corrosion resistance. However, the efficiency of the coating's barrier effect varies among different nanofillers (PE/TA < PE/TA-GO < PE/TA-GQDs < PE/TA-GO@GQDs), indicating diverse interactions in corrosive environments and different protective efficiencies along the substrate interface. It's noteworthy that while the PE/TA coating almost lost its anti-corrosion effect after 28 days of immersion, the PE/TA-GO@GQDs exhibited a resistance of 5 × 108 Ω·cm2. This highlights the crucial role of appropriate nanofiller selection in achieving significant enhancements in corrosion protection efficacy. Thirdly, due to the high reactivity and unique spatial structure of the nanohybrid, the inclusion of TA as a modifier enables the formation of hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups. These chemical bonds improve the interaction between the polymer and substrate based on material compatibility. Additionally, the addition of modified nanohybrid materials causes the surface of the coating to transition from hydrophilic to hydrophobic, which is also beneficial for corrosion protection. Overall, incorporating the nanohybrid comprising GO, GQDs, and TA as nanofillers within a waterborne epoxy matrix demonstrates their collective advantages in one coating system. This integration effectively mitigates nanofiller agglomeration within the polymer coating structure, resulting in exceptional corrosion resistance and remarkable stability.

This research explores the synthesis of nanohybrids composed of sphere-decorated sheet structures integrating GO@GQDs nanosheets. A facile one-step hydrothermal method is employed to synthesize these nanohybrids, subsequently enhancing their surface through the application of TA as a chemical functionalization agent. These nanohybrids are intended for utilization as nanofillers within waterborne epoxy coatings by a solution mixing method. The structural and morphological alterations of the nanohybrid pre- and post-functionalization are comprehensively characterized by using XRD, FT-IR, XPS, and TEM analysis. The hydrothermal procedure, alongside TA functionalization, represents suitable methodologies to accomplish the sphere-by-layer assembly of GO and GQDs. Evaluation of the corrosion protection efficacy of epoxy nanocomposite coatings loaded with TA-GO, TA-GQDs, and TA-GO@GQDs is conducted using the EIS technique. The results indicate that the corrosion protection efficiency of the coating systems follows the order of PE/TA < PE/TA-GQDs < PE/TA-GO < PE/TA-GO@GQDs. Furthermore, the findings demonstrate that the nanocomposite coatings exhibit elevated values in terms of water contact angle, surface roughness, and pull-off adhesion strength in comparison to the PE sample. Notably, nanocomposite samples containing TA-GO@GQDs exhibit notably enhanced properties. The improved performance of the epoxy/TA-GO@GQDs nanocomposite sample is ascribed to the synergistic effects of GO, GQDs, and TA, which contribute to reinforcing the barrier resistance and surface characteristics of the coatings.

Ziyang Zhou: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhengquan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology. Ranming Niu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Siyu Huang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Julie M. Cairney: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Xueling Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Eason Yi-Sheng Chen: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Additionally, they acknowledge the research facilities and technical service supported by Microscopy Australia and the University of Sydney’s Core Research Facility program.
